title: Single-ion and salt conductor polymer electrolytes based on poly(4-vinylpyridine) quaternized with poly(ethylene oxide) side chains
abstract:
Single-Ion and Salt Conductor Polymer Electrolytes
Based on Poly(4-vinylpyridine) Quaternized with
Poly(ethylene oxide) Side Chains
CHRISTIAN CHOVINO, 1 YVES FRERE, 1 PHILIPPE GRAMAIN 2
1 Institut Charles Sadron (CRM-EAHP) (CNRS-ULP), 6 Rue Boussingault, 67083 Strasbourg Cedex, France
2 Laboratoire de Chimie Applique´e, URA 1193 (CNRS), Ecole Nationale Supe´rieure de Chimie de Montpellier,
8 Rue de l’Ecole Normale, 34053 Montpellier Cedex 1, France
Received 22 November 1996; accepted 17 February 1997
ABSTRACT:
A new type of single-ion conductor with ﬁxed cation was synthesized by
spontaneous anionic polymerization of 4-vinylpyridine in the presence of short polyeth-
ylene oxide (PEO) chains as alkylating agents. These comblike polymers have low Tgs
and are amorphous with the shorter PEOs. Their conductivities are unaffected by the
nature of the anion (Br 0, ClO 0
4 , and tosylate) and are controlled by the free volume
and the mobility of the pendant cation. By comparison of the results at constant free
volume, it is shown that the charge density decreases with the increasing length of
pendant PEO demonstrating that PEO acts only as a plasticizing agent. Best conductiv-
ity results (s Å 10 05 S cm01 at 607C) are obtained with PEO side chains of molecular
weight 350. With this sample, the conductivity in the presence of various amounts of
added salt (LiTFSI) was studied. A best value of 10 04 S cm01 at 607C is obtained with
a molar ratio EO/Li of 10. It is shown that, over the range of examined concentrations
(0.2–1.3 mol Li kg 01), the reduced conductivity sr/c increases linearly with increasing
salt concentration showing that the ion mobility increases continuously. Such behavior
is quite unusual since in this concentration range a maximum is generally observed
with PEO systems. To interpret this result and by analogy with the behavior of this
type of polymer in solution, it is proposed that the conformation of these polymers in
the solid state is segregated with the P4VP skeleton more or less conﬁned inside the
dense coils surrounded by the PEO side chains. Under the inﬂuence of the increasing
salt concentration, this microphase separation vanishes progressively: The LiTFSI salt
exchanges with the tosylate anions and acts as a miscibility improver agent. q 1997
John Wiley & Sons, Inc. J Polym Sci A: Polym Chem 35: 2719–2728, 1997
Keywords:
polymer electrolyte; single-ion conductor; comblike polymer conductor;
quaternized poly(4-vinylpyridine); poly(ethylene oxide) side chains

4 samples. The comblike structure does not
favor chain entanglement, and short-range mo-
tions of ﬁxed cations within limited regions of
space are important. The charge delocalization on
the pyridinium group together with the presence
of the PEO chains which increase the dielectric
constant of the medium facilitates such motion
by decreasing the charge interactions. The weak
inﬂuence of the nature of the anion suggests that
conduction is controlled by the cation motions and
that ion pairing is still important in spite of the
steric hindrance around the cation. It is also possi-
ble that this ion pairing is reinforced by the inter-
actions of the anion with two neighboring pyridin-

absolute temperature in Figures 1 and 2. Figure
1 shows the effect of the nature of the anion for

2 the effect of the side chain length for tosylate
polysalts. The plots are curved lines rather than
linear lines except for P4VP-750 and 2000-Ts with
lower Tgs. This suggests that segmental motion
and possibly coil translation at high temperature
play an important role in ion conduction. The con-
ductivities observed are comparable to those gen-
erally obtained with pure anionic polyelectrolytes
but are higher than published values with cat-
ionic polyelectrolytes. At room temperature, val-
ues in the range 10 07 S/cm are obtained while,
at 807C, they are in the range 10 04–10 05 S/cm

In Figure 2, conductivities obtained for tosylate
samples with different side chain lengths are rep-
resented. It is ﬁrst noted that the PEO length

At 397C, conductivities increase in the order 164
õ 750 Å 2000 õ 350, while, at higher tempera-
tures such as at 847C, data extrapolation gives
the following order: 750 Å 2000 õ 350 õ 164. The
obvious explanation is the inﬂuence of Tg. At high
temperatures, far from the Tg for all the samples,
the conductivity tends to follow an Arrhenius be-
havior and varies as the density of charges, which
decreases with the increasing PEO length. At low
temperatures, the chain mobility is affected by
the Tg proximity and the conductivity varies un-
der the competitive and contradictory inﬂuence of
segmental mobility and density of charges. In or-

to demonstrate the inﬂuence of charge density,

Nothing is known on the conformation of the
polymeric coil of our sample or similar polyelectro-
lytes in the solid state, but studies in solution
demonstrate some interesting behavior, conﬁrm-
ing the above hypothesis of an internal micro-
phase separation. As recalled in the Introduction,
the synthesis of such polymer may be carried out
by quaternization of P4VP. In this case, it has
been shown that depending on the reaction tem-
perature, the quaternization ratio is limited by
the nonaccessibility of unreacted pyridine groups
due to a change in the polymer conformation from
a free-draining coil to a scarcely permeable glob-
ule.13 Globular conformation formation was con-
ﬁrmed by light scattering experiments demon-
strating polyelectrolyte behavior and the forma-
tion of dense globular aggregates with radius of
gyration less than 100 A˚ .30 Since, in the solid
state, it is mostly probable that such a conforma-
